title: Gulf Conflict Flares Again: What Dubai, Doha Flyers Should Do
excerpt: Iran's July 12 strikes on Gulf states are disrupting flights through Dubai, Doha and Abu Dhabi. Here's how to protect a trip routed through these hubs.
content:
If your itinerary connects through Dubai, Doha, Abu Dhabi or another Gulf hub in the coming days, there's a fast-moving situation you need to know about before you fly. On July 12, 2026, Iran launched missile and drone attacks on Qatar, Bahrain, Kuwait, Oman and Jordan in retaliation for a fresh round of US strikes on Iranian territory, and separately declared the Strait of Hormuz closed "until the end of US interference in this region." It is the sharpest escalation of the US-Iran conflict since it first disrupted regional air travel back in late February, and it has again pulled major long-haul stopover airports into the middle of it.

The worst of Sunday's attacks fell on Qatar, where the Ministry of Interior confirmed three people, including a child, were injured by falling debris from missile interceptions over Doha; authorities said none of the intercepted projectiles reached Hamad International Airport itself, but the country raised its threat level and urged residents to shelter in place while air-defense operations were underway. Kuwait, Bahrain and Jordan reported strikes or interceptions with limited damage, and the UAE said it detected missile threats near its borders. Separately, Yemen's Houthis struck Saudi Arabia's Abha Airport and warned airlines to avoid Saudi airspace altogether, prompting Saudia and flynas to cancel connecting flights there.

None of the major hub airports have shut down. Dubai International, Hamad International in Doha and Abu Dhabi's Zayed International are all still operating scheduled commercial service, and Emirates, Etihad, flydubai and Qatar Airways have not announced network-wide suspensions. But the disruption is real and measurable: UAE airports logged around 460 delays and 20 cancellations on July 13 alone, with Etihad and Emirates among the most affected, and Dubai specifically recorded roughly 70 delays that day according to flight-tracking data. Airlines are absorbing the disruption through longer reroutes around closed airspace, tighter connection windows and last-minute schedule changes rather than outright cancellations for most Gulf carriers.

Longer-haul carriers that don't operate their own Gulf hub have been more willing to simply pull out. Singapore Airlines has now pushed its Singapore-Dubai restart back a fifth time, to August 3, and will fly a scaled-down aircraft when it resumes. Japan Airlines has suspended its Tokyo Haneda-Doha route through September 1. KLM has extended its Amsterdam-Dubai cancellations, and Europe's aviation safety regulator, EASA, is still telling airlines to avoid Iranian, Iraqi and Lebanese airspace entirely through at least August 31. If your routing depends on one of these specific long-haul links, treat a further delay to the restart date as more likely than not.

On the government-advisory side, both Qatar and the UAE remain at the US State Department's Level 3, "Reconsider Travel," a rating that has held since early March. The US Embassy in Abu Dhabi and Consulate in Dubai canceled non-emergency consular appointments for July 13-15 and remain on ordered-departure status for non-essential staff, which is as strong a signal as an embassy sends short of a full evacuation notice.

So what should you actually do? If you have a trip touching a Gulf hub in the next two to three weeks, check your specific flight's status directly on the airline's site within 24-48 hours of departure rather than relying on a booking made earlier, since schedules are being revised day to day. If you have flexibility, avoid tight connections through Doha or Dubai and build in extra buffer, or ask your travel agent about routing through a non-Gulf hub like Istanbul instead. Confirm your travel insurance actually covers airspace closures and conflict-related delays - many standard policies exclude exactly this scenario, so check the policy wording, not just the marketing page, before you rely on it. If you're already ticketed on Emirates, Etihad, Qatar Airways or flydubai, look up their current rebooking and waiver policies, since Gulf carriers have generally offered flexible changes during past waves of this conflict. And if you or someone you know is currently in Qatar on a visa that's about to expire, note that the automatic visa-extension safety net Qatar ran earlier this year ended on June 7, so any renewal now has to go through the standard Ministry of Interior process rather than an automatic grace period. Above all, check your government's current travel advisory for Qatar and the UAE before finalizing new bookings - conditions here have changed quickly before, and there's no sign yet that this round is different.
